entry.title: Architecting a Resilient Home Network: OpenWrt, AdGuard Home, and Cloudflare Zero Trust
entry.content:
1. The Challenge: Network Rigidity and DNS Pollution

In restrictive network environments, standard ISP-provided hardware often becomes a bottleneck for power users and developers. The primary pain points I encountered were:

  • DNS Pollution & Hijacking: Unreliable domain resolution leads to connection timeouts. While tools like BrowserLeaks can estimate leaks, they are often insufficient in sophisticated environments. If an ISP utilizes DNAT (Destination NAT) to transparently hijack UDP 53 traffic, standard leak tests may return false negatives because the interception happens silently without cryptographic verification.
  • Lack of Granularity: Client-side ad-blocking (installing apps on every phone and laptop) is unscalable. I needed a network-level “sinkhole” to filter traffic for all devices—including IoT hardware that cannot run ad-blocking software.
  • Security Risks: The need to access home resources remotely without exposing dangerous ports to the public internet.

As a Computer Science student, I needed a solution that offered low-latency secure tunneling, comprehensive ad-blocking, and secure remote management.

2. The Solution: A Split-Gateway Topology

To solve this, I designed a network architecture separating Data Link/Physical layer responsibilities from Network/Application layer processing.

  • Primary Router (Infrastructure): Handles Wi-Fi broadcasting and raw PPPoE negotiation (standard consumer router duties).
  • Secondary Gateway (Intelligence): An OpenWrt-based device acting as a “Bypass Gateway” (Side Router) to handle DNS resolution, traffic shaping, and ad-blocking.

Hardware Prerequisites

  1. Primary Router: Any high-performance consumer router capable of AP mode.
  2. Secondary Gateway: A device (e.g., Raspberry Pi, NanoPi, or x86 Mini PC) capable of running OpenWrt.

3. Implementation

Phase 0: Firmware Preparation (Flashing OpenWrt)

Note: The flashing process varies significantly by hardware manufacturer. The following is a generalized workflow.

Before configuring the network, the secondary gateway must be flashed with OpenWrt to gain root access and package management capabilities.

  1. Select Hardware: Consult the OpenWrt Table of Hardware to ensure your device is supported.
  2. Unlock Bootloader: For many consumer routers (e.g., Xiaomi, Redmi), you must first exploit the stock firmware to enable SSH and unlock the bootloader.
  3. Flash Recovery Environment: It is best practice to flash a custom bootloader (like Breed or U-Boot) to prevent “bricking” the device.
  4. Install OpenWrt:
    • Download the squashfs-sysupgrade.bin specific to your target architecture (x86_64, ARM, MIPS).
    • Upload the firmware via the recovery web interface or SSH.

Phase 1: Network Topology Configuration

I configured the primary router to handle the PPPoE connection to the ISP. Crucially, I disabled DHCP on the primary router to delegate address management to the OpenWrt gateway.

  • Primary Router IP: 10.0.0.1 (Static)
  • Mode: Wireless Access Point (AP)

Next, I configured the OpenWrt gateway:

  1. Connected via Ethernet to the Primary Router (LAN to LAN).
  2. Assigned Static IP: 10.0.0.5.
  3. Configured as a Bypass Gateway (pointing its IPv4 Gateway to 10.0.0.1).

Phase 2: Environment Dependencies & OpenClash

Accessing the OpenWrt gateway via SSH, I updated the package lists and installed the necessary dependencies for traffic routing and kernel modules.

Technical Note: OpenWrt utilizes opkg for package management.

Phase 3: AdGuard Home Integration

To implement network-wide ad-blocking, I integrated AdGuard Home. This acts as a DNS sinkhole, intercepting DNS requests and blocking known tracking domains.

(Note: Ensure you download the binary matching your CPU architecture, e.g., ARM64 vs AMD64).

Phase 4: Firewall & Port Management

I configured the OpenWrt firewall (iptables wrapper) to allow traffic on the management ports for both services (Port 3000 for AdGuard Home and 9090 for OpenClash).

Phase 5: The DNS Pipeline

This is the most critical configuration to ensure no DNS leaks occur and traffic is routed correctly.

  1. OpenClash: Disabled built-in DNS sniffing to prevent conflicts.
  2. AdGuard Home:
    • Listeners: ::1:53 and 0.0.0.0:53.
    • Upstream DNS: Points to the OpenClash local DNS port (e.g., 127.0.0.1:7874).
    • DHCP Server: Enabled on AdGuard Home.
      • Gateway set to: 10.0.0.5 (The OpenWrt device).
      • Subnet: 10.0.0.0/8.

The Traffic Flow:
Client Device -> AdGuard Home (Filter Ads) -> OpenClash (Route Traffic/Tunnel) -> Internet

Phase 6: Secure Remote Access (Cloudflare Zero Trust)

To manage the home network from outside without exposing ports (and risking security breaches), I utilized Cloudflared.

  1. Installed cloudflared on OpenWrt.
  2. Created a Tunnel via the Cloudflare Zero Trust Dashboard.
  3. Mapped 10.0.0.5:80 (LuCI Interface) to a custom subdomain (e.g., admin.mydomain.com).
  4. Security Policy: Enabled Zero Trust authentication (requiring email OTP) to access the subdomain.

4. Impact and Results

By shifting from a standard ISP setup to this custom “Split-Gateway” architecture, I achieved:

  • Enhanced Privacy: Encrypted DNS resolution prevents ISP snooping and mitigates DNAT hijacking.
  • Network Efficiency: Intrusive ads are blocked at the DNS level before reaching client devices, saving bandwidth.
  • Zero-Trust Security: Administrative access is entirely hidden behind Cloudflare’s edge network, eliminating the attack surface of open ports.

entry.title: Essential VPS Setup: Securing SSH and Enabling BBR Optimization
entry.content:
When provisioning a fresh VPS, two critical configurations yield the highest return on investment: securing the access point and unthrottling the network. Changing the default SSH port significantly reduces automated brute-force attempts, while enabling the BBR congestion control algorithm drastically improves throughput on high-latency links.

Here is the standardized workflow for these initial setups.

Part 1: Hardening SSH Access

The default port 22 is the primary target for botnets. Moving SSH to a non-standard port is the most effective “low-hanging fruit” for server security.

1. Firewall Configuration (Prerequisite)

Before touching any config files, ensure your Cloud Provider’s firewall (Security Group) allows traffic on your new target port.

  • Action: Select a port between 1024 and 65535 (e.g., 19220).
  • Cloud Console: Add an Inbound Rule (TCP) for port 19220.

2. Handle Systemd Socket Activation (Ubuntu 22.04+)

On modern Ubuntu systems, SSH is often socket-activated, meaning editing sshd_config alone will not work. You must revert to the standard service mode.

3. Edit sshd_config

Safely modify the daemon configuration.

The Safety Strategy: Do not delete Port 22 yet. Add the new port below it. This allows you to connect via either port during the transition.

Save (Ctrl+O) and Exit (Ctrl+X).

4. Restart and Verify

Apply the changes and check if the daemon is listening on both ports.

5. The Connectivity Test

CRITICAL: Do not close your current terminal session. Open a new terminal window on your local machine and test the connection.

6. Finalize

Once the new connection is verified:

  1. Edit /etc/ssh/sshd_config again and comment out #Port 22.
  2. Restart SSH: sudo systemctl restart ssh.
  3. Remove the rule for Port 22 from your Cloud Provider’s firewall.

Part 2: Network Optimization (BBR)

For network optimization, we will utilize vps_optimize, an open-source shell script that automates kernel parameter tuning. It enables Google’s BBR algorithm, optimizes TCP buffers, and adjusts Fair Queue (FQ) management.

Repository: github.com/androwbrown/vps_optimize

Usage

Note: This script modifies /etc/sysctl.conf. A backup is created automatically.

  1. Download Script

  2. Execute

  3. Apply Changes

  4. Verification
    To confirm BBR is active, run the following command. The output should contain bbr.

Rollback

If you experience network instability, restore the original configuration using the backup created by the script:

entry.title: GitHub Open Source Contribution: The 10-Step Standard Workflow
entry.content:
Many developers want to contribute to open source but get stuck on the specific Git workflow. Rather than worrying about messing up someone else’s repository, simply master the standard “Forking Workflow.”

Here is the 10-step guide to contributing to any GitHub project.

1. Fork the Repository

Navigate to the target repository (e.g., github.com/remote/test) and click the Fork button in the top-right corner.
This creates a copy of the project under your personal account (github.com/me/test).

2. Clone to Local

Clone your personal copy (the fork) to your local machine.
Note: Do not clone the original author’s repository directly, or you will lack push permissions.

3. Configure Upstream

To track changes from the original repository, you must add it as a remote source, conventionally named upstream.

4. Sync Latest Code

Before writing any code, ensure your local main branch is synchronized with the original repository to avoid conflicts later.

5. Create a Feature Branch

Never commit directly to the main branch. Always create a dedicated branch for your specific task.

6. Code and Stage

Perform your development work. Once finished, add the modified files to the staging area.

7. Commit Changes

Write a clear, descriptive commit message. Adhering to Conventional Commits is highly recommended.

8. Keep Branch Updated (Optional but Recommended)

If your development took several days, the original repository may have moved ahead. Before pushing, it is best practice to rebase your branch against the latest upstream.

9. Push to Origin

Push your feature branch to your personal remote repository (Origin).

10. Open a Pull Request (PR)

  1. Go to your GitHub repository page (github.com/me/test).
  2. GitHub will usually display a banner saying “Compare & pull request”. Click it.
  3. Verify the direction:
    • Base repository: remote/test (main)
    • Head repository: me/test (feat/add-login-module)
  4. Fill in the PR description, reference any relevant Issues (e.g., “Fixes #123”), and click Create pull request.

Common Pitfalls

  • Clean .gitignore: Ensure you are not committing local IDE configurations (like .vscode, .idea) or OS files (.DS_Store).
  • PR Granularity: A PR should do one thing well. Do not combine a bug fix, a new feature, and a code refactor into a single Pull Request. This makes the code review process difficult for maintainers.

entry.title: Mastering `this` Binding and Event Delegation in React JSX
entry.content:
1. The Challenge: Context Loss in Class Components

In JavaScript Class components, managing the this context is a notorious source of confusion. While the dot notation (e.g., obj.method()) works intuitively in standard execution, passing methods as callbacks in React’s JSX often leads to “detached” references. Without proper binding, this becomes undefined during event execution, causing applications to crash.

This article analyzes the specific behaviors of arrow functions versus regular functions within the JSX event loop, using a dual-component example.

2. The Setup: Counter vs. Clocker

We assume a standard project structure where the entry point renders two distinct components: Counter (User-triggered state via Arrow Function) and Clocker (Lifecycle-triggered state via Wrapper).

3. Technical Analysis: Binding Patterns

Why must Counter.Count be an arrow function, while Clocker.tick can remain a regular method? It depends entirely on how the function is invoked.

Pattern 1: Direct Reference (Class Fields)

Syntax: <button onClick={this.Count}>

In the Counter component, we pass the function reference directly.

  • Mechanism: When React’s event system executes this callback later, it invokes the function “nakedly” (without a calling object).
  • Why Arrow Functions Work: The Count method is defined as a Class Field (Arrow Function). Arrow functions do not have their own this; they capture the this from the lexical scope (the class instance) at definition time.
  • Result: Even when called by the event system, this remains bound to the Counter instance.

Pattern 2: The Arrow Function Wrapper

Syntax: <button onClick={() => this.Count()}>

This creates an anonymous arrow function inside render.

  • Mechanism: When the button is clicked, the anonymous function executes. Inside its body, it explicitly calls this.Count().
  • Context Preservation: Since render belongs to the class instance, the this inside the anonymous function correctly points to the instance.
  • Flexibility: In this pattern, Count can be either a regular function or an arrow function, because it is being called as a method of the object (this.Count()), not as a detached reference.

4. Anti-Patterns and Pitfalls

Understanding these errors is crucial for debugging React applications.

❌ Error 1: Immediate Invocation (The Infinite Loop)

Syntax: <button onClick={this.Count()}>

  • The Bug: By adding parentheses (), you are instructing JavaScript to execute the function immediately during the render phase, rather than passing it as a callback to be executed later.
  • The Chain Reaction:
    1. render runs.
    2. this.Count() executes immediately.
    3. setState is called.
    4. React detects a state change and triggers a re-render.
    5. Go to step 1.
  • Result: Maximum update depth exceeded (Stack Overflow / Infinite Loop).

❌ Error 2: The Detached Regular Method

Syntax: <button onClick={this.Count}> (Where Count is a Regular Function)

  • The Bug: You pass the reference to a standard class method without binding.
  • The Execution: When the user clicks, React calls the function reference. Since class methods in strict mode are not bound by default, this becomes undefined.
  • Result: TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'setState').

❌ Error 3: The “No-Op” Wrapper

Syntax: <button onClick={() => this.Count}>

  • The Bug: The onClick handler executes the arrow function, which contains the statement this.Count.
  • The Execution: This statement merely accesses the property Count but does not invoke it. It essentially does nothing.
  • Correction: You must execute the function: onClick={() => this.Count()}.

5. Summary Table

SyntaxFunction TypeOutcomeNote
onClick={this.fn}Arrow Function✅ SuccessRecommended. Best for performance (created once).
onClick={this.fn}Regular Method❌ Errorthis is undefined. Requires .bind(this) in constructor.
onClick={() => this.fn()}Any✅ SuccessFlexible, but creates a new function on every render (slight perf cost).
onClick={this.fn()}Any❌ CrashCritical Error. Infinite loop due to immediate execution.

entry.title: Openwrt上通过AdguardHome和OpenClash部署私人DNS
entry.content:
摘要：

  • DNS污染与劫持
  • 分流策略的潜在风险
  • 为什么选择ASIS
  • 搭建局域网私人DNS

DNS即Domain Name Server,即域名解析服务器，默认53端口、UDP连接、明文传输，可能会受到污染、劫持和监听。

DNS

DNS解析过程

浏览器提取url中域名部分，首先查询浏览器缓存，若未命中，查询系统hosts文件，若未命中，则向出口网卡指定的DNS服务器发送请求。若未指定，则默认为为DHCP握手响应的DNS服务器，即一般是本地ISP的DNS服务器发起递归查询。本地ISP收到DNS请求后，查询缓存，若未命中，则向根（root)、顶级（TLD）及地方权威域名服务器发起迭代查询，如图1。浏览器收到响应后，根据TTL值将域名缓存到本地。

More info: Search in Google

劫持和污染

1.本地ISP出于审查和减少网间结算的目的，对DNS请求进行记录和劫持；
2.在国际入网口，GFW会按照黑名单封锁二级和三级域名，将大量域名污染到黑洞IP；
3.学校、企业等部署内网审计设备，通过上游部署旁路由，对53端口流量进行劫持和记录。
4.部分国产os会将用户指定的dns更改为受审查的dns。

More info: 维基百科：DNS污染

直接输入IP可以吗？

如果DNS遇到污染，我们直接输入IP能否正常连接？我们知道，对于HTTPS网站，TCP握手后需要先建立安全的套接字协议连接，即TLS连接。如果直接在浏览器中输入IP，浏览器会根据HTTPS协议选择默认端口443。客户端发出sni字段留空的Client Hello请求。如果这个IP托管有多个服务器（证书），由于SNI无效，服务器只能向客户端投递“缺省证书”+公钥+哈希值签名，在客户端进行证书链验证时，会出现证书配置错误，无法进行安全连接。

不推荐使用分流

我一向不主张分流策略。对于Clash-Core，分流DNS是不安全的行为。我们以Openwrt下Clash客户端为例。

OpenClash建立虚拟网卡ech0、注册ip rule，并监听0.0.0.0:53和::1:53。遇DNS请求时，OpenClash会根据域名解析策略（ip on demand/ip if not match/asls)处理这个域名。
clash DNS也有2种处理模式，我们以常见的fake ip模式为例。当没有匹配到域名规则时，OpenClash首先会去匹配fakeip缓存，其次去匹配fakeip过滤器，如果在其中，则使用nameserver直连解析。否则将会建立一项fake ip映射表，真实ip由nameserver+proxy和fallback+proxy并行查询解析，取其最快响应ip。
这会造成，如果一个网站在html中通过js嵌入一些如”*.qq.com”的简单请求时，会导致用户的代理匹配基于域名的direct规则，使用直连DNS解析为国内CDN，并通过直连连接，造成了DNS泄漏和IP泄漏。在无跨域错误的情况下，网站可获得危险（实名）的国内IP，对用户造成安全隐患。

为什么选用ASIS

若策略为ip if not match，首先clash会匹配基于域名的规则，如匹配到Direct规则，则通过直连DNS直接解析。若没有匹配到基于域名的规则，则使用本地DNS将域名解析为IP，使用IP进行匹配。如果nameserver本身包含域名，则由default nameserver充当其bootloader DNS服务器。如匹配到Proxy规则，则使用Clash DNS解析。
若策略为ip on demand，首先clash会使用本地DNS将域名解析为IP，接下来匹配基于域名和IP的规则，进行匹配。
这两种策略，都会有本地DNS泄漏的问题。

我的解决方案：OpenWrt+OpenClash+AdguardHome

通过Openwrt作旁路由，上游网关指向主路由，主路由用于拨号，盘路由上运行DHCP服务器桥接真实网卡，将网关指向旁路由的静态IP。Adguard Home监听0.0.0.0:53，开启重定向，上游DNS为CLash监听DNS端口，Clash关闭53端口劫持。
